steps.text:
Your computer organizes everything into folders (also called "directories" — same thing, different name). Folders can contain files and other folders, like nesting boxes inside boxes.

Here's what that looks like:

    /
    └── Users/
        └── yourname/
            ├── Desktop/
            ├── Documents/
            ├── Downloads/
            └── Pictures/

That tree shape is called a "path." A path is an address that tells the computer exactly where something is. For example:

    /Users/yourname/Documents

That means: start at the very top, go into Users, then yourname, then Documents. Each / separates one folder from the next.

When you use the terminal, you're always "standing inside" one folder. Think of it like being in a room — you can look around and see what's in that room, or walk into a different room.

Right now your terminal is in a folder. Let's find out which one.

## pwd — print working directory

`pwd` is a command that prints the full path of the folder you're currently in. "Working directory" just means "the folder you're in right now."

You'll see something like /Users/yourname.
steps.annotation: pwd = print working directory. This is where you are right now in the file system.

steps.text: That's your "home folder" — the main folder for your user account. Everything you create will usually go somewhere inside this folder.

## ls — list what's here

`ls` is a command that lists everything inside your current folder. You should see familiar names — Desktop, Documents, Downloads, and others.
steps.annotation: ls lists everything in your current folder — same as opening it in Finder.

steps.text: Those are the same folders you'd see if you opened Finder (on Mac) or Files (on Linux). The terminal is just another way to look at the same stuff.

## ls -la — list everything, with details

Commands can have "flags" — extra letters that change what the command does. Flags start with a dash (-).

`ls -la` uses two flags:   -l means "long format" — show details like size and date   -a means "all" — include hidden files

Hidden files start with a dot (like .zshrc). They're usually configuration files that you don't need to touch.
steps.annotation: -l means show details. -a means show hidden files (the ones starting with a dot).

steps.text: Don't worry about all those details. The main thing to notice is that there are more items than before — those are the hidden files that -a revealed.

## clear — clean up the screen

`clear` is a command that wipes everything off the screen. Your files are still there — it just removes the visual clutter so you have a fresh view. You'll use this a lot.
steps.annotation: clear wipes the screen. Your history is still there — just scrolled up.

steps.text:
## What you learned
- A path is an address like /Users/yourname/Documents
- `pwd` shows which folder you're in
- `ls` shows what's in your current folder
- `ls -la` shows everything including hidden files
- `clear` cleans up the screen
- Flags like -l and -a modify how commands work

Tip: feel free to open a separate terminal window and try these commands on your own. Practice makes them stick!
